nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; adults tend to inhabit protected reef flats in lagoons and inshore reefs at shallow depths (0–4 m)

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding primarily on filamentous algae and zooplankton. In aquariums has been reported to readily accept a variety of meaty and vegetable-based foods including Mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, Spirulina, frozen and freeze-dried preparations, flake, and pellet foods. Feeding multiple small portions per day is generally advised.

summary: The Blue Velvet Damselfish is a hardy species native to reefs of the Western Pacific. Juveniles are black with vivid electric-blue stripes, though adults typically fade to uniform dark brown. Generally considered a good fit for semi-aggressive or predator community setups.
careNotes:
The Blue Velvet Damselfish is generally considered a hardy species and is commonly recommended for hobbyists setting up semi-aggressive or predator-only communities rather than peaceful reef setups. The older scientific name Paraglyphidodon oxyodon is still widely encountered in the aquarium trade.

Color phases: Juveniles are black with vivid electric-blue horizontal stripes and a pale yellow-white vertical band. Adults are typically uniform dark brown to black, with the striking juvenile coloration largely fading as the fish matures. Adults have also been known to become increasingly territorial with age.

Temperature recommendations vary across aquarium sources and wild data, with hobby guidance generally ranging from 22–28°C. A minimum tank of around 185 liters (approximately 55 gallons) is generally advisable, and larger is preferable in community settings to give other fish adequate space to escape territorial behavior.

The Blue Velvet Damselfish is generally considered reef-safe with respect to coral and invertebrate damage, though it has been known to bully and harass more peaceful tankmates in reef setups.

Health: The species has been reported to be susceptible to ich (a common parasitic infection causing white spots on the skin), particularly under unstable water conditions. Stable parameters and consistent water quality practices are generally advised.
breedingNotes: Males have been reported to establish territories and court females with a characteristic swimming display. Spawning is oviparous (egg-laying), with eggs adhering to a substrate surface; the male guards and aerates the eggs after spawning. Captive breeding has not been documented in hobby literature, and this species is generally considered not bred in captivity under standard aquarium conditions.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6 in), temperature (72–80°F / 22.2–26.7°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), minimum tank size (55 gal). Rated reef-safe 'with caution' due to aggression toward peaceful reef fish; 3 other sources rate it reef-safe outright.

additional.name: ThatPetPlace
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6 in), temperature (75–82°F / 23.9–27.8°C), pH (8.0–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.024), minimum tank size (55 gal). Temperature range is notably higher than other hobby sources. Min salinity upper bound (1.024) slightly lower than other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous pair spawner; eggs adhere to substrate with paternal care. Captive breeding has not been documented in hobby literature.
